primary_diagnosis.name: Nodulo-ulcerative basal cell carcinoma of the right nasal ala with acute haemorrhage

differential.evidence_basis: Slowly enlarging, painless, pearly nodule on chronically sun-exposed facial skin with central ulceration and easy bleeding — the classic macroscopic picture of a nodular BCC.
Also arises on sun-exposed skin and can ulcerate and bleed — must be kept in mind until biopsy, though the pearly translucent appearance favours BCC over a hyperkeratotic, crusted SCC.
A bleeding, changing facial lesion must not have melanoma missed; the very slow two-year growth and rolled pearly borders argue against it, but only histology excludes it.
A common cause of an easily-bleeding skin nodule, but these usually erupt and grow over weeks — the two-year chronicity argues strongly against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the AI Doctor should already frame this as a suspected skin malignancy needing biopsy, not as simple traumatic bleeding.

differential.diagnosis: basal cell carcinoma (nodulo-ulcerative) with contact bleeding
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic pearly nodule, rolled telangiectatic border, central ulceration, painless, non-pruritic, on the nasal ala — a high-risk anatomical site. The bleeding is mechanical from fragile surface vessels, worsened by antiplatelet therapy.
Still not excludable without histology; the non-keratotic, pearly, slowly-growing morphology makes it less likely than BCC.
Retained on the list as the must-not-miss diagnosis; definitive exclusion is by excision biopsy, which telehealth cannot perform.
No surrounding induration, spreading erythema, warmth, purulence or fever — an acute soft-tissue infection is not supported.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen confirms a suspicious malignant lesion with controllable bleeding and no features of infection or shock.

differential.diagnosis: nodulo-ulcerative basal cell carcinoma with acute haemorrhage
differential.evidence_basis: Clinically classic BCC; definitive diagnosis still requires histology. Immediate need is hemostasis; definitive need is biopsy and excision. Melanoma and SCC remain formally on the differential until the biopsy returns.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A telehealth-appropriate answer names a suspected skin cancer requiring urgent in-person hemostasis and a biopsy/excision referral, without asserting a confirmed diagnosis and without over-escalating a stable, controllable bleed to an ambulance.

red_flags.description: Non-healing, slowly enlarging, ulcerating lesion on chronically sun-exposed facial skin — suspected skin malignancy
red_flags.clinical_significance: A chronic non-healing ulcerated facial nodule is a skin cancer until proven otherwise and requires tissue diagnosis; delay allows local tissue destruction, especially at the nasal ala.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissing it as trivial trauma leaves an untreated skin cancer to enlarge and invade local structures on the face.

red_flags.description: Active, recurrent spontaneous bleeding from the lesion in a frail elderly patient on antiplatelet therapy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Aspirin impairs platelet function so a fragile tumour vessel can ooze persistently; while currently a slow controllable venous ooze, it needs definitive hemostasis and can recur.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to control the bleed or to safety-net for recurrence risks ongoing blood loss and distress in a frail patient.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Tumours of the nasal ala have higher recurrence risk and often require specialist excision planning (e.g. Mohs / staged excision), which mandates referral rather than simple community management.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciating the site risk can lead to inadequate excision and recurrence.

pathophysiology_summary: Basal cell carcinoma arises from the basal keratinocytes of the epidermis, driven by cumulative ultraviolet damage (hedgehog pathway dysregulation), which is why it favours chronically sun-exposed sites such as the nasal ala. The nodulo-ulcerative subtype forms a pearly, translucent dome with a rolled border and prominent arborising surface telangiectasias; as the tumour outgrows its blood supply centrally it ulcerates ('rodent ulcer'). The fragile neovascular surface vessels bleed easily with minor trauma, and concurrent low-dose aspirin (irreversible platelet cyclo-oxygenase inhibition) prolongs that bleeding. BCC is locally invasive and destructive but metastasises only very rarely, so the acute risk here is local bleeding and tissue destruction rather than systemic spread. Definitive management is tissue diagnosis (biopsy) and complete excision; acute management is mechanical/chemical hemostasis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the presentation as simple traumatic bleeding of an ordinary skin lump and reassuring/discharging without arranging biopsy or referral — missing a skin cancer.
Reflexively sending a frail nursing-home resident by ambulance to ED for a slow, controllable venous ooze in a haemodynamically stable patient — an unnecessary and itself-harmful over-escalation.

Anchoring on 'bleeding facial lesion = melanoma emergency' and managing the bleed as an acutely life-threatening event, rather than recognising a controllable BCC bleed needing urgent-but-not-emergency in-person care.

Trying to 'manage it at home' definitively (or instructing home cautery/removal) instead of arranging in-person hemostasis and a biopsy/excision pathway — telehealth cannot cauterise, dress the wound, or biopsy.

Advising the patient to stop their daily low-dose aspirin to control a minor controllable ooze without weighing the cardiovascular indication — a change that should be a considered clinician decision, not a reflex.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for induration, fixation or deep extension
Histopathological diagnosis via punch biopsy or excision
Application of chemical cautery (silver nitrate) or a professional haemostatic dressing
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Direct the on-site registered nurse to apply firm, continuous, uninterrupted direct pressure over gauze for a full 20 minutes without peeking; arrange urgent same-day in-person review (facility GP visit, urgent care, or skin clinic) for definitive hemostasis and a biopsy/excision referral pathway; escalate to the emergency department only if bleeding is not controlled by sustained pressure or if signs of significant blood loss appear.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for recognising a suspicious lesion (the morphology is classic). The real risks are UNDER-recognition (dismissing a skin cancer as trivial bleeding) and OVER-triage (ambulance/ED for a controllable ooze in a stable frail patient).
